ownerText: My dog was in a hot car for a short time and now he is panting hard, drooling, and weak.
whyThisCaseMatters: Emergency heat exposure with weakness should prefer a heat or global emergency screen before generic history.

ownerText: My bulldog came back from a warm walk and is panting loudly at rest, but he is still standing.
whyThisCaseMatters: Short-nosed dog heat risk can be missed if the planner asks a broad timing question first.

ownerText: My dog played outside in the heat, vomited once, and now keeps panting even in the shade.
whyThisCaseMatters: Confusing multi-symptom case where vomiting should not pull the first question away from heat screening.

ownerText: My dog was clipped by a car, has blood on one leg, and seems shaky with pale gums.
whyThisCaseMatters: Trauma with gum-color concern should screen for shock-like red flags before wound detail.

ownerText: My dog got in a fight and has a puncture on the shoulder with a steady drip of blood.
whyThisCaseMatters: Bite wound wording should map to trauma and ask a high-value bleeding or wound question.

ownerText: My dog fell off the porch, is limping, and has a scraped bleeding paw.
whyThisCaseMatters: Confusing multi-symptom trauma plus limping should keep accident and bleeding candidates ahead of generic mobility follow-up.

ownerText: My male dog keeps squatting to pee but only a few drops come out and he cries.
whyThisCaseMatters: Little or no urine output is must-not-miss and should not be handled like routine accidents.

ownerText: My dog is having accidents inside, straining outside, and the urine looks pink.
whyThisCaseMatters: Mixed accident and blood language should still screen for output and straining.

ownerText: My dog vomited this morning and has been trying to pee all day with almost nothing coming out.
whyThisCaseMatters: Confusing multi-symptom urinary plus GI case where no urine output must outrank a generic vomiting question.

ownerText: My large dog keeps trying to vomit but nothing comes up, and his belly looks tight.
whyThisCaseMatters: Classic retching plus tight belly should go straight to the bloat screen.

ownerText: My dog is pacing, drooling, and his abdomen seems bigger than usual.
whyThisCaseMatters: Less explicit bloat language still needs an abdomen and retching discriminator.

ownerText: My dog has diarrhea, keeps gagging without bringing anything up, and his belly feels hard.
whyThisCaseMatters: Confusing multi-symptom GI wording should still prioritize bloat red-flag screening.

ownerText: My dog is breathing with his mouth open and his sides are moving hard while resting.
whyThisCaseMatters: Clear breathing distress should prefer emergency respiratory screening.

ownerText: My dog has been coughing and now his tongue looks bluish.
whyThisCaseMatters: Cough language with color change must not get a routine cough follow-up first.

ownerText: My dog was outside earlier and now is breathing very fast on the floor, but he is not vomiting.
whyThisCaseMatters: Confusing multi-symptom respiratory plus heat case should still screen current breathing first.

ownerText: My dog fainted for a moment and now is very weak and wobbly.
whyThisCaseMatters: Collapse should be screened as must-not-miss even if the dog is awake now.

ownerText: My dog suddenly cannot walk straight and his gums look almost white.
whyThisCaseMatters: Weakness with pale gums should prioritize circulation red flags.

ownerText: My dog vomited twice and then became too weak to stand without help.
whyThisCaseMatters: Confusing multi-symptom vomiting plus weakness should not start with a generic GI-only question.

ownerText: My dog had uncontrolled shaking for several minutes and seems dazed now.
whyThisCaseMatters: Long shaking episode should screen seizure duration and emergency neuro flags.

ownerText: My dog had two shaking episodes today and stumbled after the second one.
whyThisCaseMatters: Multiple neuro episodes need clustering and collapse red-flag screening.

ownerText: My dog dropped to the floor, twitched a little, then got up confused.
whyThisCaseMatters: Confusing collapse versus seizure wording should still choose neuro or collapse emergency discriminators.

ownerText: My dog ate dark chocolate from a bag and has vomited once.
whyThisCaseMatters: Known exposure should keep toxin screening ahead of routine vomiting characterization.

ownerText: My dog may have chewed a box of rodent bait in the garage, but I am not sure how much.
whyThisCaseMatters: Uncertain amount of suspected toxin should still screen exposure first.

ownerText: My dog chewed a houseplant and now is drooling and walking wobbly.
whyThisCaseMatters: Confusing multi-symptom exposure plus neuro-like signs should keep toxin exposure in the first screen set.

ownerText: My dog has vomited four times since this morning and seems tired.
whyThisCaseMatters: Repeated vomiting should ask frequency or hydration stability before a broad intake question.

ownerText: My dog has diarrhea with red streaks and vomited once overnight.
whyThisCaseMatters: Blood in stool should be captured as a GI red flag rather than buried after generic duration.

ownerText: My dog drinks water and it comes back up soon after, and he has loose stool.
whyThisCaseMatters: Inability to keep water down is a high-value first question candidate in GI cases.

ownerText: My dog has raised bumps spreading over his body after a bee sting.
whyThisCaseMatters: Spreading hives after a sting should screen allergy emergency flags before routine skin location.

ownerText: My dog keeps licking his paws and scratching his belly, and the skin looks red.
whyThisCaseMatters: Routine itchy skin should prefer localizing and appearance questions, not a generic wellness prompt.

ownerText: My dog has a swollen muzzle, itchy bumps, and vomited once after eating something new.
whyThisCaseMatters: Confusing multi-symptom allergy plus GI case must screen facial swelling and hives first.

ownerText: My dog is holding his back leg up and will not put weight on it.
whyThisCaseMatters: Non-weight-bearing lameness should ask the weight-bearing discriminator first.

ownerText: My dog jumped off the couch and immediately started toe-touching on one front leg.
whyThisCaseMatters: Sudden onset after a jump needs mobility and trauma-onset candidates.

ownerText: My dog is limping after running through brush and has a small cut between the toes.
whyThisCaseMatters: Confusing multi-symptom lameness plus wound should avoid a generic pain question and keep mobility first.
